name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىِ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (1)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَرْفَعُوٓا۟ أَصْوَٰتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا۟ لَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (٢)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet, and do not speak aloud to him like you shout to one another, lest your works should fail without your being aware. (2)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَٰتَهُمْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱمْتَحَنَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٣)
```

```english
Indeed those who lower their voices in the presence of the Apostle of Allah—they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for Godwariness. For them will be forgiveness and a great reward. (3)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَآءِ ٱلْحُجُرَٰتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤)
```

```english
Indeed those who call you from behind the apartments, most of them do not use their reason. (4)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
```

```english
Had they been patient until you came out for them, it would have been better for them, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٦)
```

```english
O you who have faith! If a vicious character brings you some news, verify it, lest you should visit [harm] on some people out of ignorance, and then become regretful for what you have done. (6)
```

```arabic
وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَزَيَّنَهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ وَٱلْفُسُوقَ وَٱلْعِصْيَانَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلرَّٰشِدُونَ (٧)
```

```english
Know that the Apostle of Allah is among you. Should he comply with you in many matters, you would surely suffer. But Allah has endeared faith to you and made it appealing in your hearts, and He has made hateful to you faithlessness, transgression and disobedience. It is such who are the right-minded— (7)
```

```arabic
فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (٨)
```

```english
a grace and blessing from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (8)
```

```arabic
وَإِن طَآئِفَتَانِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَإِنۢ بَغَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ فَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى تَبْغِى حَتَّىٰ تَفِىٓءَ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن فَآءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَأَقْسِطُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (٩)
```

```english
If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them. But if one party of them aggresses against the other, fight the one which aggresses until it returns to Allah’s ordinance. Then, if it returns, make peace between them fairly, and do justice. Indeed Allah loves the just. (9)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers and be wary of Allah, so that you may receive [His] mercy. (10)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah contains matters of the laws of religion (شرائع الدين) through which the happy life of the individual is completed and the righteous, wholesome order is established in society. Some of these pertain to the beautiful etiquette (أدب جميل) of the servant with Allah, the Glorified, and with His Messenger, as seen in the first five verses at the opening of the Surah. Others relate to the human being with his peers in terms of their existence within the vital society. Still others relate to the differentiation between individuals, which is among the most important factors by which civil society is organized and which guides man to a happy life and a wholesome, pleasant existence. Through this, the religion of truth (دين الحق) is distinguished from other legal social systems and otherwise. The Surah concludes by pointing toward the reality of faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام) and His, the Exalted, bestowal of favor through the light of faith that He pours forth.

The Surah is Medinan (مدنية), as evidenced by the contents of its verses, except for what has been said regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى** "O mankind! Indeed, We created you from a male and a female" (49:13), which will be discussed later.

His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** "O you who have faith! Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle, and be wary of Allah. Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" (49:1). "Between the hands of a thing" (بين يدي الشيء) means in front of it; this is a common metaphorical (مجازي) or figurative (استعاري) usage. Attributing this to both Allah and His Messenger together, rather than to the Messenger alone, is evidence that it is a matter shared between Him, the Exalted, and His Messenger. This is the station of judgment (مقام الحكم) which belongs exclusively to Allah, the Glorified, and to His Messenger by His permission, as He, the Exalted, said: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah" (12:40), and He said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** "We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave" (4:64).

Evidence for this is the opening of the prohibition with His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "O you who have faith!" and its conclusion with His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** "and be wary of Allah. Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing." This clearly indicates that what is meant by "between the hands of Allah and His Messenger" is the station that links the God-conscious (المتقين) believers to Allah and His Messenger, which is the station of judgment (مقام الحكم) from which they derive their creedal (الاعتقادية) and practical (العملية) rulings.

From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: **لا تُقَدِّمُوا** "Do not venture ahead" is to not put forward any judgment in opposition to the judgment of Allah and His Messenger—either by rushing to a statement (القول) before receiving the word regarding it from Allah and His Messenger, or to an action (الفعل) before receiving the command for it from Allah and His Messenger. However, His, the Exalted, concluding the prohibition with His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** "Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" is more suited to putting forward a statement rather than an action, or rather than a general sense encompassing both statement and action. Otherwise, it would have been said: "Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing" (سميع بصير), so that "All-hearing" would correspond to the statement and "All-seeing" to the action, as He, the Exalted, does in many instances regarding actions with phrases like: **وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ** "and Allah sees best what you do" (57:4). Thus, the sum of the meaning is: do not judge in matters where Allah and His Messenger have a ruling except after the ruling of Allah and His Messenger; that is, do not judge except by the judgment of Allah and His Messenger, and let the mark (السمة) of following (الاتباع) and emulation (الاقتفاء) be upon you.

However, considering that every act or omission by a human being is not devoid of a ruling concerning it, and likewise the resolve (العزم) and will (الإرادة) toward an act or omission, then acts, omissions, their will, and the resolve upon them all enter into the ruling of following (الاتباع). The prohibition against "venturing ahead of Allah and His Messenger" implies a prohibition against initiating or proceeding toward a statement that has not been heard from Allah and His Messenger, and toward an act, omission, resolve, or will regarding either of them before receiving the judgment from Allah and His Messenger. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His, the Exalted, saying regarding the description of the angels: **بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ** "Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture ahead of Him in speech, and they act by His command" (21:26-27).

This following (الاتباع) called for by His saying: **لا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** "Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle" is the entry into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, standing in the position of servitude (العبودية), and walking in its path by the servant making his will (مشيته) subordinate to the will of Allah in the stage of legislation (التشريع), just as it is subordinate to it in the stage of creation (التكوين). He, the Exalted, said: **وَما تَشاؤُنَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ** "But you do not wish unless it be that Allah wishes" (76:30), and He said: **وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** "and Allah is the Guardian of the faithful" (3:68), and He said: **وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ** "and Allah is the Guardian of the God-wary" (45:19).

The commentators have several views regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **لا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ**:

One is that "putting forward" (التقديم) means "going ahead" (التقدم), so it is intransitive (لازم). The meaning of "Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Messenger" is: do not be hasty in commanding and forbidding without Allah and His Messenger, and do not decide upon a command or prohibition without Allah and His Messenger. It has also been said that "putting forward" in the verse carries its well-known [transitive] meaning, but it is used by omitting its objects, like His saying: **يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ** "He gives life and He causes death" (57:2). Thus, the meaning boils down to simply something being in front of another, returning to the meaning of "going ahead."

The wording is absolute (مطلق), encompassing going ahead in statement or action, even going ahead of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in walking or sitting, or preceding with timed acts of worship before their time, and so on.

Another view is that the intent is the prohibition of speaking before the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); that is, if you are in his assembly and he is asked about something, do not precede him with the answer until he answers first.

Another is that the meaning is: do not precede him in statement or action until he commands you to do so.

Another is that the meaning is: do not put your statements and actions ahead of the statement and action of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and do not allow anyone to walk in front of him.

It appears that interpreting **لا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** as a prohibition against venturing ahead of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) only, in these last three views, is based on their treating the mention of Allah, the Exalted, alongside His Messenger in the verse as a type of honoring (تشريف), like saying: "Zayd and his generosity pleased me." Thus, His, the Exalted, mention is to indicate that preceding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is, in any case, in the sense of preceding Allah, the Glorified.

Perhaps reflection on the view we previously presented is sufficient to prevent one from adopting any of these other views.

His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** "and be wary of Allah. Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" is a command for godwariness (التقوى) in the position of following and servitude. There is no circumstance for a human being except the circumstance of servitude (العبودية); therefore, He mentioned godwariness in an absolute sense.

In His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ** "Indeed, Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing" is the justification (تعليل) for the prohibition and the godwariness within it. That is: be wary of Him by refraining from this prohibited act; do not put forward a statement with your tongues nor in your secrets, for Allah is All-hearing (سميع), hearing your statements, and All-knowing (عليم), knowing your outward and your inward, your public and your secret.

His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْواتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِ** "O you who have faith! Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet..." etc. This means that their voices, when addressing and speaking to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), should not be higher than his voice or louder. This is because, as has been said, it involves one of two things: either a type of belittling (استخفاف) him, which is disbelief (الكفر), or a breach of etiquette (إساءة الأدب) regarding his station, which is contrary to the veneration (التعظيم) and respect (التوقير) that has been commanded.

His saying: **وَلا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ** "and do not speak aloud to him like you shout to one another" (49:2). Part of veneration during conversation is that the speaker's voice be lower than the voice of the one addressed. Thus, absolute loudness (الجهر) in address lacks the meaning of veneration. Addressing great personages with loudness, as one addresses common people, is not devoid of a breach of etiquette and impudence (الوقاحة).

His saying: **أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمالُكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "lest your works should fail while you are unaware" (49:2). That is: so that your works do not fail, or out of dislike that your works should fail. This is connected to both prohibitions together. That is: We have only prohibited you from raising your voices above his voice and speaking aloud to him as you do to one another so that your works do not become void (تبطل) thereby while you are unaware, for in these two acts lies the failure of works (الحبط). We have already discussed the failure of works (الحبط) — *the total nullification of the spiritual rewards of one's deeds* — in the second volume of this book.

Some have allowed that **أَنْ تَحْبَطَ** "lest [they] fail" etc., is a justification for the prohibited act itself (the raising and shouting). The meaning would be: your doing that for the sake of failure is prohibited. The difference between justifying the prohibition (النهي) and justifying the prohibited act (المنهي عنه) is that on the first view, the prohibited act is justified, and on the second, the justified act is prohibited. This involves an obvious strained interpretation (تكلف).

The apparent meaning of the verse is that raising the voice above the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) voice and speaking aloud to him are two sins that necessitate the failure of works (الحبط). Thus, there are sins other than disbelief (الكفر) that cause the failure of works.

The verse has also been interpreted to mean that the failure (الحبط) intended is the loss of the reward for that specific deed itself, not the nullification of the rewards of all other deeds as is the case with disbelief. It is said in *Majma' al-Bayan*: "Our companions have said: The meaning in His saying: **أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمالُكُمْ** is that the reward for that specific deed fails. For if they had performed it in a manner of venerating and respecting the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), they would have deserved reward. When they performed it in a manner contrary to that, they deserved punishment and missed that reward, so their work failed. Thus, the people of 'the Threat' (أهل الوعيد) have no basis in this verse. Furthermore, He, the Exalted, linked the failure in this verse to the work itself, while they link it to the merit deserved for the work, and that is contrary to the apparent meaning." End quote.

The response to this is that the failure (الحبط) related to disbelief—which undoubtedly relates to the reward of all deeds—is also linked in His speech to the deeds themselves, as in this verse. Therefore, this should be understood in the same way that was understood, without difference. The claim that it is contrary to the apparent meaning is rejected, for the voiding (بطلان) of a deed is the voiding of its effect that follows from it.

The verse has also been interpreted by building on the premise that failure (الحبط) is exclusive to disbelief, arguing that raising the voice above the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) voice and speaking aloud to him are not causes of failure in themselves, but rather in terms of their sometimes leading to offending (إيذاء) him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and offending him is disbelief, and disbelief causes the failure of works.

Some have said: The intent in the verse is the prohibition of raising the voice absolutely. It is known that its rationale is to guard against what is expected therein of offending the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which is disbelief that causes the failure of works by consensus. Thus, the prohibition was issued against what is a likely cause of offense—whether that meaning is present or not—to protect the sacred precinct and to cut off the source.

Then, since this prohibited act is divided into that which reaches the level of disbelief—which is that which offends him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and that which does not reach that level, and there is no evidence to distinguish one category from the other, and even if its existence were assumed, it would often be ignored, it became necessary for the person of legal responsibility (المكلف) to refrain from it absolutely for fear of falling into that which causes the failure of works, which is that which reaches the level of offense.

The ambiguity of one category with the other is indicated by His, the Exalted, saying: **أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمالُكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "lest your works should fail while you are unaware." Otherwise, if raising the voice and speaking aloud were prohibited absolutely, whether they reached the level of offense or not, there would be no place for His, the Exalted, saying: **وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "while you are unaware." For the matter would be confined to either the raising of the voice or speaking aloud reaching the level of offense, thus being disbelief and certainly causing failure, or not reaching it, thus also being a sin and certainly causing failure. Thus, failure would be certain in any case, so there would be no place for qualifying the speech with the lack of awareness, while awareness is established absolutely due to knowledge of it after the prohibition. End of summary.

The response to this is that the appearance of His saying: **لا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْواتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِّ وَلا تَجْهَرُوا لَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ** as an intrinsic prohibition (نهي نفسي) rather than a preliminary prohibition (نهي مقدمي) taken as a precaution is beyond doubt. However, each of these two acts is something whose nature as an evil deed is perceived by the intellect (عقلا) before the arrival of the religious prohibition against it, like fabrication (الافتراء) and slander (الإفك). Those who were committing them were the believers, as the prohibition began with His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "O you who have faith!" While they might be lenient regarding some evil deeds, considering them trivial, they would not accept the nullification of their faith and their righteous deeds (الصالحات) from their very root.

Thus, the Glorified alerted them with His saying: **أَنْ تَحْبَطَ أَعْمالُكُمْ وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** to the fact that you do not realize the enormous and great effect this has, for it is nothing less than the failure of works. So do not approach anything of the two, lest your works fail while you are unaware.

So His saying: **وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** "while you are unaware" looks at their state before the prohibition, where they were aware of the act being an evil deed but did not know the magnitude of its evil to this extent. As for after the issuance of the divine explanation, they are aware of the failure.

Thus, the verse from one perspective is similar to His, the Exalted, saying in the verses of slander: **وَتَحْسَبُونَهُ هَيِّناً وَهو عِنْدَ اللهِ عَظِيمٌ** "and you deem it a trivial matter, while it is a grave matter with Allah" (24:15), and His saying in the verses of the Resurrection: **وَبَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ** "and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned" (39:47).

His, the Exalted, saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْواتَهُمْ عِنْدَ رَسُولِ اللهِ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ امْتَحَنَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوى** "Indeed those who lower their voices in the presence of the Apostle of Allah—they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for God-wariness..." etc. (49:3). Lowering (غض) the voice is the opposite of raising it. The meaning of testing (الامتحان) is trial and examination, and it is only done to obtain knowledge of the state of a thing previously unknown. Since this is impossible in His, the Exalted, case, what is meant here is training and habituation—as has been said—or placing trial and hardship upon the heart so that it becomes accustomed to godwariness (التقوى).

The verse is presented as a beautiful promise for lowering the voice in the presence of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), after describing them as those whose hearts are tested for godwariness, and the one who tested them for that is Allah, the Glorified. In this is a confirmation and strengthening of the content of the previous verse and an encouragement to refrain from what was prohibited therein.

In referring to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in this verse as "the Messenger of Allah" (رسول الله) after referring to him in the previous verse as "the Prophet" (النبي), there is an indication of the basis of the ruling. For the Messenger, insofar as he is a messenger, has no authority of his own; what belongs to him belongs to the One who sent him. Venerating and respecting him is veneration and respect for the One who sent him.

Thus, lowering the voice in the presence of the Messenger of Allah is veneration and magnification of Allah, the Glorified. Persistence and continuity in that—as understood from His saying: **يَغُضُّونَ** "they lower," which implies continuity—reveals their being characterized by godwariness and His, the Exalted, testing of their hearts for godwariness.

His saying: **لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** "For them is forgiveness and a great reward" (49:3) is a beautiful promise to them in exchange for the godwariness of Allah in their hearts, and the ultimate outcome is for godwariness.

His, the Exalted, saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُنادُونَكَ مِنْ وَراءِ الْحُجُراتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ** "Indeed those who call out to you from behind the chambers, most of them do not exercise their reason" (49:4). The context of the verse indicates that this was an actual occurrence, and that they were a group of coarse people who used to call out to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from behind the chambers (الحجرات) of his house without observing the requirements of etiquette and the duty of veneration and respect. So Allah, the Glorified, censured them by describing most of them as not exercising their reason, like the beasts among animals.

His, the Exalted, saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا حَتَّى تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكانَ خَيْراً لَهُمْ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** "Had they been patient until you could come out to them, it would have been better for them. And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (49:5). That is: had they been patient instead of calling you, and not called you until you came out to them, it would have been better because of the good etiquette and the observance of veneration and respect for the station of the Message (الرسالة). That would have brought them closer to the forgiveness and mercy of Allah, for He is All-forgiving, All-merciful.

So His saying: **وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** "And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" is as if looking at the mentioned patience. It is also possible that it looks at the fact that most of them do not exercise their reason, meaning: what issued from them of ignorance and bad etiquette is forgiven because it was not out of their reason and understanding, but rather out of a deficiency in that, and Allah is All-forgiving, All-merciful.

His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ جاءَكُمْ فاسِقٌ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوا** "O you who have faith! If a vicious person brings you any news, verify it..." etc. (49:6). The **vicious person** (الفاسق) — *one who has departed from obedience into disobedience* — and the **news** (النبأ) — *a report of great significance*. **Verification** (التبين), *seeking clarity* (الاستبانة), and *making clear* (الإبانة) — according to *al-Sihah* — carry the same meaning; they can be transitive or intransitive. When transitive, they mean clarification and making manifest; it is said: "I verified the matter" (تبينت الأمر), meaning I clarified it and made it manifest. When intransitive, they mean becoming clear and manifest; it is said: "The matter became clear" (أبان الأمر), meaning it became manifest and evident.

The meaning of the verse is: O you who have faith! If a vicious person brings you a significant report, then verify his report by searching and examining to stand upon its reality, for fear that you might visit a people with ignorance and then become regretful for what you did to them.

Allah, the Glorified, has endorsed in this verse the principle of acting upon a report, which is one of the rational principles (الأصول العقلائية) upon which the foundation of human social life is built. He commanded verification regarding the report of a vicious person, which is in the sense of prohibiting acting upon his report. Its reality is the disclosure of the lack of consideration for its authority (حجية). This is also like an endorsement of what the rational people (العقلاء) have built upon regarding the lack of authority of a report where the one reporting it is not trusted, and the lack of acting upon its effect.

The explanation for this is: human life is a cognitive life (حياة علمية) in which one builds his conduct and path of life upon what he witnesses of good and evil, benefit and harm, and the opinion he adopts therein. This is not possible for him except in what is within his sight and presence. What is absent from him regarding his life and livelihood is more than what is present, so he is forced to complete the knowledge he has with the knowledge others have, obtained through witnessing and observation. There is no way to this except through hearing, which is the report (الخبر).

Thus, relying on a report—in the sense of acting upon its effect in deed and transaction—is treated as the knowledge obtained by a human being through witnessing and observation in general, upon which human social life depends in a primary way. Upon this, the foundation of the rational people and the pivot of action rest.

So the report, if it is widely transmitted (متواترا) or surrounded by definitive circumstantial evidence (قرائن قطعية) that necessitates the certainty of its content, is a considered authority (حجة معتبرة) without hesitation. If it is not widely transmitted nor surrounded by that which provides certainty of its content—which is termed "the solitary report" (خبر الواحد) in technical terms—then what is considered among them is that which is trustworthy (الموثوق به) according to its type, even if it does not provide it according to its specific instance. All of this is because they do not act except upon what they see as knowledge, which is either real knowledge or trust and reassuring conjecture (الظن الاطمئناني) that is customarily counted as knowledge.

Once this is established, His, the Exalted, saying in justifying the command for verification in the report of a vicious person: **أَنْ تُصِيبُوا قَوْماً بِجَهالَةٍ** "lest you should visit [harm] on a people out of ignorance" etc., implies that what is commanded is the removal of ignorance and the attainment of knowledge regarding the content of the report when one intends to act upon it and arrange its effect. Thus, in the verse is the affirmation of what the rational people affirmed and the negation of what they negated in this chapter; it is an endorsement (إمضاء), not an initiation (تأسيس).

His, the Exalted, saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ اللهِ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ** "And know that the Apostle of Allah is among you. Had he obeyed you in many a matter, you would have surely suffered..." etc. (49:7). **Suffering** (العنت) — *sin and destruction*. **Compliance** (الطوع) and **obedience** (الطاعة) — *submission*. However, obedience is most often said regarding following what is commanded and adhering to what is prescribed, as mentioned by al-Raghib. But sometimes the matter is reversed, so the superior's following what the subordinate wants and desires is called obedience from the superior to the subordinate. From this is His, the Exalted, saying in the verse: **لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ** "Had he obeyed you," where the Messenger's acting according to what the believers see and desire is called obedience from him to them.

The verse, as the context indicates, is a continuation of the speech in the previous verse, generalizing its ruling and confirming its justification. The content of the previous verse is the ruling of the obligation of verification in the report of a vicious person and its justification by the obligation of guarding against building action upon ignorance. The content of this verse is alerting the believers to the fact that Allah, the Glorified, has brought them into the law of right-guidance (شرع الرشد); therefore, He made faith beloved to them, beautified it in their hearts, and made disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience hateful to them. They must not be heedless of the fact that the Messenger of Allah is among them, and he is supported by Allah and is upon a clear proof from his Lord, walking only the path of right-guidance, not error. They must obey the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in what he commands them, desire what he desires, and choose what he chooses. They should not insist that he obey them in their opinions and desires, for if he were to obey them in many a matter, they would suffer and perish.

So His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ اللهِ** "And know that the Apostle of Allah is among you" is conjoined to His saying in the previous verse: **فَتَبَيَّنُوا** "verify." The advancement of the predicate is to indicate exclusivity (الحصر) and to point to its necessary implication. For their being distinguished by the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being among them necessitates that they attach themselves to right-guidance, avoid error, refer matters to him, obey him, and follow his tracks, and not attach themselves to what their desires demand of them.

The meaning is: do not forget that the Messenger of Allah is among you. This is an allusion (كناية) to the fact that they must refer matters and proceed in the events they face according to what he sees and commands, without following the desires of their own souls.

His saying: **لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ** "Had he obeyed you in many a matter, you would have surely suffered," meaning you would have struggled and perished. The sentence is like an answer to a hypothetical question, as if a questioner were asking: "Why do we refer to him and he does not refer to us nor agree with us?" So it was answered that "Had he obeyed you in many a matter, you would have surely suffered."

His saying: **وَلكِنَّ اللهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمانَ وَزَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ** "but Allah has made faith dear to you and beautified it in your hearts" (49:7) is an exception (استدراك) from what the previous sentence **لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ** indicates—that they are naturally on the verge of destruction and error. So He excepted that Allah, the Glorified, rectified that by the blessing He bestowed upon them of making faith beloved and making disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience hateful.

What is meant by making faith beloved to them is making it a beloved thing to them, and by beautifying it in their hearts is adorning it with a beauty that attracts their hearts to itself, so they attach themselves to it and turn away from what distracts them from it.

His saying: **وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْكُفْرَ وَالْفُسُوقَ وَالْعِصْيانَ** "and He has made disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience abhorrent to you" (49:7) is conjoined to "made dear" (حَبَّبَ). Making disbelief and what follows it abhorrent to them is making them hated things to them from which their souls recoil. The difference between viciousness (الفسوق) and disobedience (العصيان)—as has been said—is that viciousness is the departure from obedience into sin, and disobedience is the sin itself, or if you wish, say: all sins. It has also been said: what is meant by viciousness is lying, by the context of the previous verse, and disobedience is the rest of the sins.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ هُمُ الرَّاشِدُونَ** "It is they who are the right-minded" (49:7) is an explanation that the love of faith and the attraction to it, and the hatred of disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience, is the cause of the right-guidance (الرشد) that a human being seeks by his primordial nature (الفطرة) and from whose opposite, error (الغي), he recoils. So the believers must adhere to faith and avoid disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience so that they may be right-guided and follow the Messenger and not follow their desires.

Since the love of faith and attraction to it, and the hatred of disbelief and its like, was a quality of some of those among whom the Messenger was, not all of them—as the previous verse explicitly states—and He described their group as a whole with that to preserve their unity and to encourage those among them who were not so characterized, the context changed and turned from addressing them to addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), saying: **أُولئِكَ هُمُ الرَّاشِدُونَ** "It is they who are the right-minded." The reference is to those characterized by the love of faith and the hatred of disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience, so that it may be a praise for those so characterized and an encouragement for others.

Know that in His saying: **وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ اللهِ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ** there is an intimation that a group of the believers were insistent on accepting the report of the vicious person referred to in the previous verse, who was al-Walid ibn 'Uqbah. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent him to Banu al-Mustaliq to collect their zakat. He came to them, but when he saw them, he feared them and returned to Medina, informing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that they had apostatized. So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) resolved to fight them, then the verse was revealed and he turned back, while among the people were some who insisted on raiding them.

The story will follow in the subsequent discussion of narrations.

His, the Exalted, saying: **فَضْلاً مِنَ اللهِ وَنِعْمَةً وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ** "[This is] a grace from Allah and a bounty, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise" (49:8) is a justification for what preceded of His, the Exalted, act with the believers in making faith beloved and beautified, and making disbelief, viciousness, and disobedience hateful. That is: that was from Him, the Exalted, as a mere gift and blessing, not in exchange for anything reaching Him from them. However, it is not a random act, for He, the Exalted, is All-knowing (عليم) of the recipient of His gift and blessing, All-wise (حكيم), not doing what He does randomly, as He said: **وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوى وَكانُوا أَحَقَّ بِها وَأَهْلَها وَكانَ اللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيماً** "and He made them abide by the word of God-wariness, and they were worthier of it and deserving of it, and Allah has knowledge of all things" (48:26).

His, the Exalted, saying: **وَإِنْ طائِفَتانِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ اقْتَتَلُوا فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما** "If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them..." (49:9) until the end of the verse. Fighting (الاقتتال) and mutual fighting (التقاتل) carry the same meaning. The return of the plural pronoun in **اقْتَتَلُوا** "they fight" to the two groups is in consideration of the meaning, for each of the two groups is a collective, and their sum is a collective, just as the return of the dual pronoun to them is in consideration of the wording.

It is narrated from some regarding the reason for the distinction between the two pronouns: that they are first, in the state of fighting, mixed together, so the pronoun was pluralized first. In the state of peace, they are distinct and separate, so the pronoun was dualized.

His saying: **فَإِنْ بَغَتْ إِحْداهُما عَلَى الْأُخْرى فَقاتِلُوا الَّتِي تَبْغِي حَتَّى تَفيءَ إِلى أَمْرِ اللهِ** "Then if one of them wrongs the other, fight the one which wrongs until it returns to Allah’s ordinance" (49:9). **Wronging** (البغي) — *oppression and transgression without right*. **Returning** (الفيء) — *the act of returning*. What is meant by "Allah's ordinance" (أمر الله) is what Allah has commanded. The meaning is: if one of the two groups transgresses against the other without right, then fight the transgressing group until it returns to what Allah has commanded and submits to His judgment.

His saying: **فإِنْ فاءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما بِالْعَدْلِ** "Then if it returns, make peace between them with justice" (49:9). That is: if the transgressing group returns to Allah's ordinance, then make peace between them, but not a peace by merely laying down arms and ceasing the fight, but rather a peace characterized by justice (العدل) by implementing the rulings of Allah regarding what the transgressor transgressed in terms of blood, honor, property, or any other right that was lost.

His saying: **وَأَقْسِطُوا إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ** "and act equitably. Indeed Allah loves the equitable" (49:9). **Acting equitably** (الإقساط) — *giving everyone what they deserve of their portion and share, which is justice*. Thus, conjoining His saying: **وَأَقْسِطُوا** to His saying: **فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما بِالْعَدْلِ** is the conjoining of the absolute to the restricted for emphasis. His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ** is a justification providing emphasis upon emphasis, as if it were said: make peace between them with justice and be just always and in all matters, because Allah loves the just for their justice.

His, the Exalted, saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ** "The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers..." (49:10). This is a new sentence (استئناف) confirming what preceded regarding making peace between the two fighting groups of believers. Restricting the relationship between the believers to the relationship of brotherhood (الإخوة) is a premise prepared for justifying the ruling of peace in His saying: **فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ**. It implies that the two fighting groups, due to the existence of brotherhood between them, must have peace established between them, and the peacemakers, because they are brothers to the two fighting groups, must strive to reconcile what is between them.

His saying: **فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ** "Therefore, make peace between your brothers" and He did not say: "Therefore, make peace between the two brothers," is among the most concise and elegant of speech. It implies that the two fighting groups have brotherhood between them, so it is obligatory that peace be established between them, and the rest of the believers are brothers to the two fighting groups, so they must strive for reconciliation between them.

His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ** "and be wary of Allah, so that you may receive His mercy" (49:10) is an exhortation (موعظة) for both the fighting groups and the peacemakers together.

## A Discourse on the Meaning of Brotherhood (الإخوة)

Know that His saying: **إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ** "The faithful are indeed brothers" is a legislative establishment (جعل تشريعي) of the relationship of brotherhood between the believers, which has legislative effects and established rights. It has already been mentioned in some previous discussions that among fatherhood, sonship, brotherhood, and other types of kinship, there are those that are conventional (اعتباري) and established, which religions and laws consider for the purpose of arranging specific effects upon them, such as inheritance, maintenance, the prohibition of marriage, and so on. Others are natural (طبيعي) by ending at a single loin or a single womb, or both.

The conventional type of kinship is different from the natural type. They may coincide, like two brothers born to a man and a woman from a legitimate marriage. They may also differ, like a natural child born of adultery; he is not a child in Islam and is not linked to his progenitor, even if he is a natural child. Likewise, the adopted child (الداعي) is a child in some laws but is not a natural child.

The consideration of the conventional meaning, even if it is for the purpose of arranging the effects of its reality upon it—as one of the people is taken as a head (رأسا) for them so that his relationship to them is like the relationship of the head to the body, thus he manages the affairs of the society and judges between them and among them just as the head judges over the body.

However, since the convention was for a necessitating interest, it was subordinate to that interest. If the interest necessitated arranging all the effects of the reality, they would all be arranged upon it. If it necessitated some of them, then what is arranged upon the conventional subject is that portion. Just as the recitation (القراءة), for example, is a part of the prayer, and the real part is such that the whole is absolutely negated by its negation; however, the prayer is not negated by the negation of the recitation if that was due to forgetfulness, and the prayer is only invalidated if it is omitted intentionally.

For this reason also, the effects of a conventional meaning may differ according to different instances, like the partiality of the bowing (الركوع), where the prayer is invalidated by its addition or omission intentionally or forgetfully, unlike the partiality of the recitation as previously mentioned. Thus, it is permissible for the effects arranged upon a conventional meaning to differ according to different instances. However, conventional effects are only arranged upon a conventional subject; for example, a human being disposes of his property, but not insofar as he is a human being, but insofar as he is an owner (مالك). A brother inherits from his brother in Islam not because he is a natural brother sharing the deceased's father or mother or both—for the child of adultery is likewise but does not inherit from his natural brother—but rather he inherits from him because he is a brother in the Islamic law (الشريعة).

Brotherhood is of this sort. Some of it is natural brotherhood that has no effect in religions and laws, which is the sharing of two humans in a father or a mother or both. Some of it is conventional brotherhood that has conventional effects. In Islam, it is: lineage brotherhood (أخوة نسبية) which has effects in marriage and inheritance; foster brotherhood (أخوة رضاعية) which has effects in marriage but not inheritance; and religious brotherhood (أخوة دينية) which has social effects but no effect in marriage and inheritance.

The saying of Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) will follow: "The believer is the brother of the believer; he is his eye and his guide; he does not betray him, nor oppress him, nor deceive him, nor make him a promise and then break it."

This meaning has been hidden from some commentators, so they took the absolute application of brotherhood in His, the Exalted, speech regarding the believers as a metaphorical application (إطلاق مجازي) by way of metaphor (استعارة), by likening the sharing in faith to the sharing in the origin of birth, because each of them is an origin for survival—since birth is the source of life, and faith is the source of eternal survival in the Gardens. It has also been said: it is by way of eloquent simile (تشبيه بليغ) in terms of their attribution to a single origin, which is faith that necessitates eternal survival.

## Discussion of Narrations

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His, the Exalted, saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "O you who have faith!": Zurarah narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said: "No swords were drawn, nor were ranks formed in prayer or in battle, nor was the call to prayer (الأذان) proclaimed aloud, nor did Allah send down: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** until the sons of the tribes of al-Aws and al-Khazraj had submitted to Islam."

I say: It is also narrated from Ibn 'Abbas: "**يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** was only revealed in Medina, and **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ** was only revealed in Mecca."

The report continues. Some have hesitated regarding the generality of its conclusion. Know that there are narrations in *al-Durr al-Manthur* and *Tafsir al-Qummi* regarding the occasion of revelation (سبب نزول) of His saying: **لا تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيِ اللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** which do not correspond to the verse with that level of correspondence; we have left them, and whoever wishes to stand upon them should refer to those two works.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Ya'la, al-Baghawi in *Mu'jam al-Sahabah*, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Anas, who said: "When **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَرْفَعُوا أَصْواتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ النَّبِيِ** until His saying **وَأَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ** was revealed—and Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas was loud-voiced—he said: 'I am the one who used to raise my voice above the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); my work has failed and I am among the people of the Fire.' He sat in his house grieving."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) missed him, so some of the people went to him and said to him: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has missed you; what is the matter with you?" He said: "I am the one who raises my voice above the voice of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and speaks aloud to him; my work has failed and I am among the people of the Fire." They came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of that, and he said: "No, rather he is among the people of Paradise." When the day of al-Yamamah came, he was killed.

I say: his saying "When the day of al-Yamamah came, he was killed" is from the words of the narrator, meaning he was martyred on the day of al-Yamamah, so that was the confirmation of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying. The narration is reported through other various chains with slight differences.

In it: al-Bukhari narrated in *al-Adab*, and Ibn Abi al-Dunya and al-Bayhaqi from Dawud ibn Qays, who said: "I saw the chambers (الحجرات) made of palm branches, covered from the outside with haircloth. I think the width of the door from the door of the chamber to the door of the house was about six or seven cubits, and the interior of the house was ten cubits, and I think its height was between seven and eight." I say: something like its beginning was narrated from Ibn Sa'd from 'Ata' al-Khurasani, who said: "I reached the chambers of the wives of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—made of palm branches with haircloth of black hair on their doors." The narration continues.

In it: Ahmad, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mandah, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a good chain from al-Harith ibn Dirar al-Khuza'i, who said: "I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and he called me to Islam, so I entered into it and affirmed it. He called me to the zakat and I affirmed it. I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, I will return to my people and call them to Islam and the payment of zakat. Whoever responds to me, you shall send to me, O Messenger of Allah, a messenger at such and such a time to bring you what I have collected of the zakat.'"

When al-Harith had collected the zakat from those who responded to him, and the time arrived when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended to send to him, the messenger was delayed and did not come. al-Harith thought that some displeasure from Allah and His Messenger had occurred regarding him. He called the nobles of his people and said to them: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had set a time for me to send his messenger to receive what I had of the zakat, and there is no breaking of promise from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). I do not see the delay of his messenger except due to his displeasure. So let us go and come to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had sent al-Walid ibn 'Uqbah to al-Harith to receive what he had collected of the zakat. When al-Walid had traveled until he reached part of the way, he became afraid. He returned and came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "al-Harith refused me the zakat and intended to kill me." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) dispatched an expedition to al-Harith.

al-Harith approached with his companions until he met the expedition as it was leaving Medina. al-Harith met them and they said: "This is al-Harith." When he reached them, he said to them: "To whom were you sent?" They said: "To you." He said: "And why?" They said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent al-Walid ibn 'Uqbah to you, and he claimed that you refused him the zakat and intended to kill him." He said: "No, by the One who sent Muhammad with the truth, I did not see him nor did he come to me."

When al-Harith entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he said: "Did you refuse the zakat and intend to kill my messenger?" He said: "No, by the One who sent you with the truth, I did not see him nor did he see me. I only approached when your messenger was delayed from me, fearing that there had been displeasure from Allah and His Messenger." Then **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ جاءَكُمْ فاسِقٌ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوا** until His saying **حَكِيمٌ** was revealed.

I say: the revelation of the verse regarding the story of al-Walid ibn 'Uqbah is widely transmitted (مستفيض) through the paths of both the Sunnis and the Shi'a. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr said in *al-Isti'ab*: "There is no disagreement among the people of knowledge regarding the interpretation of the Qur'an, as far as I know, that His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنْ جاءَكُمْ فاسِقٌ بِنَبَإٍ** was revealed regarding al-Walid ibn 'Uqbah."

In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain to Ziyad al-Hadhdha' from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a narration of his, he said: "O Ziyad, woe to you! Is the religion anything but love? Do you not see the saying of Allah: **إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ** 'If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins' (3:31)? Or do you not see the saying of Allah to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمانَ وَزَيَّنَهُ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ** 'He has made faith dear to you and beautified it in your hearts'? He said: **يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ** 'they love those who have migrated to them' (59:9). And he said: Love is the religion and the religion is love."

I say: it is narrated in *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Fudayl ibn Yasar from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), what is in its meaning, and its wording is: "Is faith anything but love and hate?" Then he recited this verse: **حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ الْإِيمانَ** until the end of the verse.

In *al-Majma'*: "It is said: viciousness (الفسوق) is lying—from Ibn 'Abbas and Ibn Zayd, and it is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

I say: there are some other narrations with this meaning.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to 'Ali ibn 'Uqbah from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The believer is the brother of the believer; he is his eye and his guide; he does not betray him, nor oppress him, nor deceive him, nor make him a promise and then break it."

I say: there are other narrations from him (peace be upon him) with its meaning, and in some of them: "The Muslim is the brother of the Muslim; he does not oppress him, nor forsake him, nor backbite him."

In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain to Abu Hamzah al-Thumali from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "The believer is the brother of the believer by his father and mother. That is because Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, created the believer from the clay of the Gardens of the heavens, and flowed into them from the wind of His Spirit; therefore, he is his brother by his father and mother."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Anas, who said: "It was said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'If only you would come to 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy.' So he went and rode a donkey, and the Muslims went walking, and it was a salty land. When he went to them, he ['Abdullah] said: 'Get away from me! For by Allah, the smell of your donkey has offended me.'"

A man from the Ansar said: "By Allah, the donkey of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has a more pleasant smell than you!" Men from his people became angry for 'Abdullah, and the companions of each of them became angry for them. There was striking between them with palm branches, hands, and sandals. Then **وَإِنْ طائِفَتانِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ اقْتَتَلُوا فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَهُما** was revealed regarding them.

I say: in some narrations, as in *al-Majma'*, the one who said that to 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul was 'Abdullah ibn Rawahah, and the clash occurred between his clan from al-Aws and the clan of 'Abdullah ibn Ubayy from al-Khazraj. The correspondence of the verse in its subject and ruling to these narrations is obscure.
